1:
"After these things Paul departed from Athens, and came to Corinth." The witnesses note Paul's move from Athens to Corinth. The puritan Henry and the witnesses observe the transition from the philosophic city to the commercial and dissolute Corinth — the great city of Achaia, notorious for its wealth, luxury, and vice.

The grammatical voices note the contrast. Athens, where Paul had reasoned with the philosophers (ch. 17) with modest success, gives way to Corinth, where a great church would be founded. The move marks a new and fruitful stage of the second journey.

The Reformed Calvin (under v.2) notes the providence in Paul's circumstances at Corinth, where he found lodging with the exiled Aquila — God turning a base entrance into a great harvest. The verse sets the scene for the eighteen-month ministry at Corinth (v.11).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul departed Athens and came to Corinth — moving from the philosophic city to the wealthy, dissolute commercial center of Achaia (Henry), where a great church would be founded. The verse displays the providential transition: the modest results at Athens give way to the abundant harvest at Corinth. The verse marks the beginning of one of the most significant ministries of Paul's life: the eighteen months at Corinth, where, despite the city's vice, the gospel would triumph and a great church would be gathered. The verse, brief as it is, opens a major chapter in the apostolic mission: Paul's arrival at Corinth, the crossroads of the Roman world, where he would labor longer than almost anywhere else. The contrast with Athens is instructive — the gospel found more fruit among the dissolute Corinthians than among the proud Athenian philosophers. The verse sets the stage for the meeting with Aquila and Priscilla (v.2), the tentmaking, the synagogue ministry, the opposition, the vision, and the long, fruitful stay. Paul's coming to Corinth marks the founding of a church to which he would write his great epistles, a church gathered out of one of the most corrupt cities of the ancient world — a testimony to the power of the gospel to save even there.
2:
"And found a certain Jew named Aquila, born in Pontus, lately come from Italy, with his wife Priscilla; (because that Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome:) and came unto them." The witnesses note Paul's meeting with Aquila and Priscilla and the expulsion of the Jews from Rome. On the expulsion, the grammatical voices cite Suetonius. Barnes: Suetonius records that Claudius "expelled the Jews from Rome, who were constantly exciting tumults under their leader, Chrestus" — possibly a mistaken reference to Christ, the disturbances arising from the preaching of the gospel among the Jews at Rome (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).

On Aquila and Priscilla, the witnesses note they became Paul's friends and fellow-workers (Romans 16:3; 1 Corinthians 16:19). Robertson notes Priscilla's name often comes first, suggesting her prominence. Whether already Christians or converted through Paul, they became his hosts and partners.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the providence: "Paul found no more fit host than a man that had been banished out of his own country... this happy calamity of Aquila doth teach us, that the Lord doth often better provide for us when he doth sharply punish us." The exile of Aquila became the means of his meeting Paul.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul found Aquila and Priscilla, Jews lately expelled from Rome by Claudius's edict — an edict Suetonius attributes to tumults under "Chrestus," possibly a garbled reference to Christ and the disturbances over the gospel (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson). Aquila and Priscilla became Paul's hosts, friends, and fellow-workers (Romans 16:3), Priscilla often named first (Robertson). Calvin draws the providence: Aquila's exile, a calamity, became the means of his meeting Paul — God providing better through affliction. The verse displays the providence that brought Paul together with this devoted couple: the expulsion from Rome, by God's hand, led them to Corinth and to Paul. The verse marks the beginning of one of the great friendships of the apostolic age: Aquila and Priscilla, who would host Paul, work with him, instruct Apollos (v.26), and serve the church in Rome and Ephesus. The verse displays both the historical detail (Claudius's edict, attested by Suetonius) and the providence behind it: the exile that seemed a calamity brought Aquila to the apostle, and gave the church two of its most faithful servants. God's hand, even in the edict of a pagan emperor, gathered his people for his purposes.
3:
"And because he was of the same craft, he abode with them, and wrought: for by their occupation they were tentmakers." The witnesses note Paul's tentmaking and self-support. On the trade, the grammatical voices note the dignity of labor. Barnes: "The apostle was not ashamed of honest industry for a livelihood; nor did he deem it any disparagement that a minister of the gospel should labour with his own hands." Paul worked to support himself (Acts 20:34; 1 Thessalonians 2:9).

On the Jewish custom of learning a trade, the witnesses note the rabbinic principle. Robertson cites Rabbi Judah: "He that teacheth not his son a trade, doth the same as if he taught him to be a thief." Wesley: "it was a rule among the Jews... to bring up all their children to some trade, were they ever so rich or noble." Even the learned Paul had a trade.

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul's reason for self-support: "the false apostles taught freely without taking any thing, that they might craftily creep in, therefore the holy man would not give place to them... lest he should cause the gospel of Christ to be evil spoken of" (1 Corinthians 9:12, 15). Paul worked to keep the gospel free from reproach.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, being of the same trade (tentmaking), lodged and worked with Aquila and Priscilla — not ashamed of honest labor for a livelihood (Barnes), following the Jewish custom of every man learning a trade (Robertson, Wesley, the rabbinic principle). Calvin notes Paul's reason: to keep the gospel free from reproach, refusing support lest the false apostles slander him (1 Corinthians 9). The verse displays the dignity of labor and the apostle's self-support: Paul, the learned rabbi, worked with his hands to preach the gospel without charge. The verse marks the model of the tentmaking minister: Paul supporting himself by his trade, that the gospel might be free. The verse displays both the Jewish wisdom of learning a trade and the apostolic principle of self-support: Paul would not burden the churches nor give the false apostles occasion to malign him. The verse honors honest labor: the apostle was not above working with his hands, and his trade became the means of his ministry and his testimony. Paul's tentmaking, far from a disparagement, displays his devotion: he labored by day and preached by night, that the gospel might advance without cost or reproach.
4:
"And he reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath, and persuaded the Jews and the Greeks." The witnesses note Paul's synagogue ministry at Corinth. The grammatical voices note the method. Clarke: "Discoursed at large concerning Jesus as the Messiah, proving this point from their own Scriptures." Paul reasoned every sabbath, persuading Jews and the Greek God-fearers who attended.

On "persuaded," the witnesses note the conative force. Robertson: "Imperfect active, conative, he tried to persuade both Jews and Greeks (God-fearers who alone would come)." Paul labored to convince them that Jesus is the Christ.

The witnesses note the diligence of the apostle: laboring at his trade on weekdays (v.3) and reasoning in the synagogue every sabbath. Gill: "we may observe the diligence, industry, and indefatigableness of the apostle." Paul used every opportunity to preach.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath, persuading Jews and Greeks — discoursing on Jesus as the Messiah, proving it from the Scriptures (Clarke), trying to convince them (the conative imperfect, Robertson). The verse displays Paul's diligent synagogue ministry: laboring at his trade by day, reasoning in the synagogue on the sabbath (Gill). The verse marks the regular method of Paul's mission: beginning in the synagogue, reasoning from the Scriptures, persuading both Jews and the Greek God-fearers. The verse displays the apostle's industry and method: using every sabbath to preach Christ from the Old Testament, addressing both Jews and Gentiles. The synagogue was Paul's regular starting-point, where he could reason with those who knew the Scriptures and the God-fearers drawn to the synagogue. The verse displays the foundation of the Corinthian church: Paul's weekly reasoning, persuading Jews and Greeks that Jesus is the Christ. Until the opposition arose (v.6), the synagogue was the base of his ministry; and even his initial preaching there gathered the first converts. Paul's diligence — working and preaching, weekday and sabbath — displays the model of the faithful minister, sparing no effort to win souls. The verse displays the apostle as the model of the bivocational minister: working at his trade through the week, yet never neglecting the pulpit of the synagogue on the sabbath. His reasoning was not mere assertion but argument drawn from the Scriptures the Jews professed to revere, meeting them on their own ground and pressing the Messianic prophecies home. The double audience — Jews and the Greek God-fearers drawn to the synagogue — foreshadows the mixed church soon to be gathered at Corinth.
5:
"And when Silas and Timotheus were come from Macedonia, Paul was pressed in the spirit, and testified to the Jews that Jesus was Christ." The witnesses note the arrival of Silas and Timothy and Paul's intensified preaching — with a textual debate. On the variant, the witnesses divide. The Textus Receptus reads "pressed in the spirit" (constrained inwardly); but the better MSS read "constrained by the word." Clarke and Ellicott and Robertson favor "the word": Robertson: "Paul held himself together or completely to the preaching." The arrival of Silas and Timothy, with gifts from Macedonia (2 Corinthians 11:9), freed Paul to devote himself wholly to preaching.

On the arrival, the witnesses note the help and encouragement. Ellicott notes they brought a good report of the Thessalonians (1 Thessalonians 3:6). Robertson: "set Paul free from tent-making for a while so that he began to devote himself with fresh consecration to preaching."

On "testified that Jesus was Christ," the witnesses note Paul's witness. Robertson: "Paul's witness everywhere." Now, more openly and fully, Paul testified that Jesus is the Messiah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when Silas and Timothy came from Macedonia, Paul was constrained — and the witnesses divide on the text: "pressed in the spirit" (TR) vs the better-attested "constrained by the word" (Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson) — to testify that Jesus is the Christ. The arrival, with gifts from Macedonia (2 Corinthians 11:9), freed Paul from tentmaking to preach with fresh consecration (Robertson). The verse displays the intensification of Paul's preaching: relieved by his companions and their support, he gave himself wholly to testifying that Jesus is the Messiah. The verse marks the turning-point at Corinth: the arrival of Silas and Timothy, the freeing of Paul for full-time preaching, and his bold, open testimony to Jesus as the Christ. The textual variant (the word vs the spirit) does not change the substance: Paul, whether inwardly pressed or constrained by the word, devoted himself with new intensity to the gospel. The verse displays the value of fellow-workers and support: with Silas and Timothy and the Macedonian gifts, Paul could labor undistracted, pressing the testimony of Christ upon the Jews. The arrival of his companions marks a new fervor in the Corinthian ministry.
6:
"And when they opposed themselves, and blasphemed, he shook his raiment, and said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles." The witnesses note the Jews' opposition and Paul's solemn turning to the Gentiles. On "shook his raiment," the witnesses note the symbolic act. Barnes: "an expressive act of shaking off the guilt of their condemnation." Ellicott and Clarke compare Nehemiah 5:13 — a gesture of detestation and a sign that God would shake them off.

On "your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean," the witnesses note the prophetic disclaimer. Ellicott and Calvin cite Ezekiel 33:4-8: the watchman who warns is clean of the blood of those who perish. Robertson: "a solemn disclaimer of responsibility." Paul, having faithfully preached, is guiltless of their destruction.

On "I will go unto the Gentiles," the witnesses note the local application. Ellicott: "the words have a limited and local application. The Apostle did not renounce all future work among the Jews, but gave up preaching to those at Corinth." As at Antioch (13:46), Paul turns from the rejecting Jews to the Gentiles.

The witnesses agree: when the Jews opposed and blasphemed, Paul shook his raiment (a gesture of detestation, like Nehemiah 5:13, Barnes, Clarke) and declared "your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean" — the prophetic disclaimer of the watchman (Ezekiel 33, Ellicott, Calvin, Robertson), Paul being guiltless of their destruction having faithfully warned them. He turns to the Gentiles — locally, giving up the Corinthian synagogue, not renouncing all Jews (Ellicott, as at 13:46). The verse displays the solemn turning-point: the Jews' rejection, Paul's disclaimer of responsibility, and his turning to the Gentiles. The verse marks the watchman's faithfulness: Paul, having preached the gospel and been rejected, shakes off the guilt and turns to those who will hear. The verse displays the seriousness of rejecting the gospel: the blood of the rejecters is on their own heads, the faithful preacher clean. Paul's turning to the Gentiles at Corinth, like his turning at Antioch, displays the pattern of the mission: the gospel offered first to the Jews, and when rejected, carried to the Gentiles. The solemn gesture and words mark both Paul's faithfulness and the Jews' culpability — the watchman clean, the rejecters condemned by their own choice.
7:
"And he departed thence, and entered into a certain man's house, named Justus, one that worshipped God, whose house joined hard to the synagogue." The witnesses note Paul's move to the house of Justus. The puritan Henry notes Paul changed his place of preaching, not his lodging: "he went to this man's house, not to lodge, for he continued with Aquila and Priscilla, but to preach." Driven from the synagogue, Paul preached in Justus's house.

On Justus, the witnesses note he was a God-fearer (a Gentile worshipper of the true God). Henry: "he was one that worshipped God; he was not an idolater, though he was a Gentile." His house became the new meeting-place.

On the house joining the synagogue, Henry notes the significance: "I rather think it was done in charity, to show that he would come as near to them as he could, and was ready to return to them if they were but willing to receive his message." Paul preached next door, still reaching out to the Jews.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul moved his preaching to the house of Justus, a God-fearer (a Gentile worshipper of the true God), whose house adjoined the synagogue — Paul changing his place of preaching, not his lodging (still with Aquila and Priscilla, Henry). The house next to the synagogue, Henry suggests, was chosen in charity, that Paul might remain near the Jews, ready to receive any who would hear. The verse displays the adaptation of the apostle: driven from the synagogue, he preached in a private house, still reaching out to the Jews. The verse marks the relocation of the Corinthian ministry: from the synagogue to Justus's house, next door, where the gospel continued. The verse displays both the resilience of the mission (Paul preached wherever he could) and his continuing concern for the Jews (preaching next door, ready to receive them). The move to Justus's house, far from abandoning the Jews, kept the gospel within their reach — a testimony to Paul's charity even toward those who had rejected him. The verse displays the providence of a willing host: Justus, the God-fearer, opened his house, and the gospel found a new home next to the synagogue, from which the great Corinthian harvest would be gathered.
8:
"And Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue, believed on the Lord with all his house; and many of the Corinthians hearing believed, and were baptized." The witnesses note the conversion of Crispus and many Corinthians. On Crispus, the witnesses note his prominence. Barnes: he was "one of the few whom Paul baptized with his own hands" (1 Corinthians 1:14). The conversion of the synagogue ruler was a signal triumph, leaving the Jews inexcusable (Henry).

On "with all his house," the witnesses note the household conversion. Robertson: "Another household conversion, for Crispus believed in the Lord with all his house." The whole family believed and were baptized.

On "many of the Corinthians," the witnesses note the harvest in the dissolute city. Barnes: "Perhaps the power of the gospel was never more signal than in converting sinners in Corinth... if a church, under the power of Christian truth, could be organized there, it is adapted to any city." Ellicott names some converts (Gaius, Stephanas, 1 Corinthians 1:14-16). The gospel triumphed at Corinth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Crispus, the synagogue ruler, believed with all his house — a signal triumph that Paul honored by baptizing him himself (1 Corinthians 1:14, Barnes) — and many Corinthians believed and were baptized. The household conversion (Robertson) and the harvest in dissolute Corinth display the power of the gospel: if a church could be founded there, it can be founded anywhere (Barnes). The verse displays the fruit of Paul's ministry at Corinth: the synagogue ruler converted, his whole house, and many of the Corinthians. The verse marks the founding of the Corinthian church: a great harvest gathered out of a corrupt city, including its most prominent Jew. The verse displays the power of the gospel to save even in the most dissolute place: Corinth, notorious for its vice, became the site of a flourishing church. The conversion of Crispus, the synagogue ruler, was a public testimony that left the unbelieving Jews without excuse (Henry); and the conversion of many Corinthians displays the breadth of the harvest. The verse encourages all ministry in difficult places: the gospel that triumphed at Corinth can triumph anywhere, for there is no city too corrupt for the power of Christ to save.
9:
"Then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision, Be not afraid, but speak, and hold not thy peace:" The witnesses note the vision encouraging Paul. On the vision and "be not afraid," the witnesses note Paul's discouragement. Barnes: "Perhaps Paul might have been intimidated by the learning, refinement, and splendour of Corinth... To this he may allude in 1 Corinthians 2:3: 'I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling.'" The Lord met his fear with encouragement.

On the recurrence of visions, Ellicott notes: "the recurrence of these visions at each great crisis of the Apostle's life." The Lord spoke to Paul at his conversion, in the temple, at Troas, and now at Corinth — comparing his discouragement to Elijah's (1 Kings 19) and Jeremiah's.

The Reformed Calvin notes the necessity of overcoming fear: "nothing is more contrary to the pure and free preaching of the gospel than the straits of a faint heart... those only are rightly prepared to teach to whom it is granted with boldness to overcome all danger" (2 Timothy 1:7). The command "speak, and hold not thy peace" charges Paul to preach plainly and fully.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision — "be not afraid, but speak" — meeting his discouragement (the fear and trembling he confesses, 1 Corinthians 2:3, Barnes). The vision is one of several at the crises of Paul's life (Ellicott), his discouragement like Elijah's and Jeremiah's. Calvin notes the necessity of overcoming fear for the free preaching of the gospel (2 Timothy 1:7): the command charges Paul to speak plainly and fully. The verse displays the Lord's tender care for his discouraged servant: meeting Paul's fear with a vision of encouragement. The verse marks the divine encouragement at Corinth: the Lord, knowing Paul's weakness and the gathering opposition, commands him not to fear but to keep preaching. The verse displays both the reality of the apostle's discouragement (even Paul knew fear and trembling) and the Lord's gracious remedy: a vision charging him to speak boldly. The command "hold not thy peace" guards against the temptation of discouraged silence — the danger that fruitless words and gathering danger might tempt Paul to fall silent. The Lord's "be not afraid, but speak" is the charge to every discouraged minister: to overcome fear and keep preaching, trusting the Lord's presence and promise (v.10).
10:
"For I am with thee, and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee: for I have much people in this city." The witnesses note the Lord's promise of presence and protection, and the doctrine of election. On "I am with thee," the witnesses note the personal application of the general promise. Ellicott: "The general promise given to the Church at large, 'Lo! I am with you always' (Matthew 28:20), received a personal application." The Lord's presence is Paul's encouragement.

On "I have much people in this city," the witnesses note the doctrine of election. Barnes: "God had a design or purpose to save many of that people... There is no chance; no hap-hazard; no doubt in regard to his gathering them to himself." The Reformed Calvin reads it of the foreknown elect: "the Lord calleth those his people, who, though they might then for good causes be counted strangers, yet because they were written in the book of life... whom the Lord determined shortly after to gather to himself, those doth he take for his people in respect of their future faith" (John 17:6). Wesley too: "So he prophetically calls them that afterward believed."

The witnesses note the encouragement this gave Paul. Barnes: "This is the ground of encouragement to the ministers of the gospel. Had God no purpose to save sinners, they could have no hope in their work." The certainty of God's elect people emboldened Paul to preach.

The witnesses agree: the Lord promised "I am with thee" (the general promise, Matthew 28:20, personally applied, Ellicott) and protection, "for I have much people in this city" — the doctrine of election. The "much people" are the foreknown elect, not yet converted but written in the book of life (Calvin, John 17:6), whom God would gather through Paul's preaching; Wesley too reads it of those who would afterward believe. This certainty of God's elect emboldened Paul (Barnes): had God no purpose to save, the minister could have no hope. The verse displays the foundation of evangelistic hope: God has a people to be saved, certain to be gathered. The verse marks the great encouragement at Corinth: the Lord's presence, protection, and the assurance of much people to be won. The verse displays the doctrine of election as the ground of bold preaching: God's elect, foreknown and certain, will be gathered through the means of the gospel. The "much people" not yet converted are already God's in his purpose — and this certainty drove Paul to preach with confidence in dissolute Corinth. The verse teaches that the minister labors not in uncertainty but in the assurance that God has a people to save; the elect will be gathered, and the preacher is the means.
11:
"And he continued there a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them." The witnesses note Paul's long stay at Corinth. The grammatical voices note the unusual length. Barnes: "Paul was not accustomed to remain long in a place... his stay at Corinth was caused by his success, and by the necessity of placing a church... on a firm foundation." Eighteen months was a long ministry for Paul.

On the labor of founding a church, the witnesses note the difficulty. Calvin: "we gather what a hard and laborious matter the edifying of the Church is, seeing that the most excellent workmaster spent so much time about the laying of the foundation of one church only." The Corinthian church required patient, sustained labor.

The witnesses note the extension beyond the city. Ellicott and Robertson note the work reached the port of Cenchrea (Romans 16:1) and all Achaia (2 Corinthians 1:1). The eighteen months founded not only the Corinthian church but a wider work.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul continued eighteen months at Corinth, teaching the word — an unusually long stay (Barnes), caused by his success and the need to establish the church on a firm foundation. The labor of founding a church was hard and sustained (Calvin: the great workman spent so long laying one foundation); and the work extended beyond the city to Cenchrea and all Achaia (Ellicott, Robertson). The verse displays the patient, sustained ministry at Corinth: a year and a half of teaching, founding and establishing the church. The verse marks the long Corinthian ministry, encouraged by the vision (vv.9-10): Paul, assured of God's people, labored eighteen months to gather and establish them. The verse displays the difficulty and the reward of founding a church: the patient labor of teaching the word, establishing a flourishing church in a corrupt city. The eighteen months at Corinth, longer than almost any other ministry of Paul, displays both the resistance of the soil and the abundance of the harvest. The verse teaches the value of sustained ministry: not hasty visits but patient teaching, laying a firm foundation. Paul's long stay, obedient to the vision and confident in God's people, founded the great Corinthian church to which he would write his epistles — the fruit of patient, faithful labor.
12:
"And when Gallio was the deputy of Achaia, the Jews made insurrection with one accord against Paul, and brought him to the judgment seat," The witnesses note the proconsul Gallio and the Jewish assault — and the chronological anchor. On Gallio, the grammatical voices note his identity. Gill: "Gallio, who was now deputy of it, was brother to L. Annaeus Seneca, the famous philosopher... a very modest man, of a sweet disposition, and greatly beloved." Henry: "deputy of Achaia, that is, proconsul." Gallio's proconsulship provides a key date for Pauline chronology (about A.D. 51-52).

On the Jewish assault, the witnesses note the concerted attack. The Jews, emboldened perhaps by the new proconsul, made insurrection "with one accord" against Paul, bringing him to the tribunal (the bema, the judgment seat).

The witnesses note the providence in the timing. The Jews hoped the new Roman governor would condemn Paul; but Gallio's refusal (vv.14-16) would establish, in effect, the legal toleration of the gospel throughout the empire. The verse sets the scene for the famous Gallio judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when Gallio (Seneca's brother, a mild and beloved man, Gill) was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews made a concerted assault on Paul, bringing him to the judgment seat (the bema). Gallio's proconsulship is a key chronological anchor for Paul's life (about A.D. 51-52). The Jews, perhaps emboldened by the new governor, hoped to have Paul condemned. The verse displays the rising opposition and the legal crisis: the Jews bringing Paul before the Roman tribunal. The verse marks the famous Gallio episode: the Jewish assault, the appeal to the proconsul, and the judgment that would, in effect, establish the legal toleration of Christianity. The verse displays the providence in the timing: the Jews' attack, intended to crush the gospel, would issue in Gallio's refusal to judge religious matters — a precedent protecting the gospel throughout the empire. The mention of Gallio, the cultured brother of Seneca, anchors the narrative in Roman history and provides one of the firmest dates in the life of Paul. The verse sets the stage for the great judgment: the Jews bringing Paul before a proconsul who would see through their charge and dismiss it.
13:
"Saying, This fellow persuadeth men to worship God contrary to the law." The witnesses note the Jews' charge against Paul — and debate which law. On "contrary to the law," the witnesses divide. Gill notes it might mean the Roman law (forbidding new gods without the senate's leave) or the law of Moses. Ellicott and Robertson hold it must mean Roman law: Ellicott: "the Jews must have meant, not the law of Moses, but that of Rome. Their contention was that... Judaism as such was still a relligio licita... Their charge against the Apostle was that he was preaching a new religion, which was not so recognised."

The grammatical voices note the legal strategy. Robertson: "To prove that Paul was acting contrary to Roman law... these Jews had to show that Paul was making converts in ways that violated the Roman regulations." The charge was that Paul taught an unlicensed religion.

The witnesses note the insidiousness of the charge. Clarke: the Jews intended to accuse Paul of violating both the Roman and the Jewish law — "He is setting up a worship of his own, in opposition to all laws." The charge aimed to make Paul appear a dangerous innovator.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews charged Paul with persuading men to worship God "contrary to the law" — and the witnesses hold this must mean Roman law (Ellicott, Robertson): the Jews claimed Judaism was a licensed religion (religio licita), and accused Paul of preaching a new, unlicensed one. The legal strategy was to show Paul violated Roman regulations on making converts (Robertson). The verse displays the Jews' legal cunning: framing the charge to bring Paul under Roman law. The verse marks the accusation before Gallio: that Paul taught an illegal religion. The verse displays the insidiousness of the charge: by claiming Paul preached an unlicensed worship, the Jews sought to have the Roman governor condemn him. The whole case turned on whether Christianity was a new religion or a form of Judaism (the licensed religion); and Gallio's judgment (vv.14-15) would treat it as the latter — a form of Judaism, not subject to his court. The verse sets up the crucial legal question whose resolution would protect the gospel: was Christianity a new, illegal religion, or a recognized form of Judaism?
14:
"And when Paul was now about to open his mouth, Gallio said unto the Jews, If it were a matter of wrong or wicked lewdness, O ye Jews, reason would that I should bear with you:" The witnesses note Gallio's interruption before Paul could speak. The grammatical voices note the phrase "about to open his mouth." Ellicott: "The phrase always implies the beginning of a set discourse. St. Paul was about to begin a formal apologia. This, however, proved to be unnecessary." Gallio cut in before Paul needed to defend himself.

On "a matter of wrong or wicked lewdness," the witnesses note the legal terms. Robertson: "Wrong (a legal wrong to the state)... wicked villainy (a crime, act of a criminal)." Ellicott: "a matter of crime or fraud." Gallio distinguishes real crimes (which he would judge) from religious questions (which he would not).

On "reason would that I should bear with you," the witnesses note the conditional. Robertson: "according to reason I should have put up with you" — a second-class condition (unfulfilled), meaning the Jews had no real legal case. Gallio would hear a genuine crime, but not this.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: before Paul could begin his defense (the formal apologia, Ellicott), Gallio interrupted — distinguishing real crimes ("wrong or wicked lewdness," legal wrong or fraud, Robertson, Ellicott), which he would judge, from the religious question before him, which he would not. The conditional ("reason would that I should bear with you") implies the Jews had no real legal case (Robertson). The verse displays Gallio's clear-headed judgment: seeing at once that the charge was not a matter for his court. The verse marks the proconsul's intervention: cutting off the trial before Paul needed to speak, distinguishing crime from religious dispute. The verse displays the wisdom of the Roman judge: he would bear with a genuine criminal complaint, but recognized that the Jews' charge was no such thing. Gallio's distinction — between real wrongs (which the state punishes) and religious questions (which it does not) — is the principle that would protect the gospel: the state has no business judging matters of doctrine and worship. The verse displays Paul's vindication without even speaking: Gallio saw through the charge, and the apostle needed no defense. The proconsul's clear-sighted refusal to be made the tool of Jewish malice marks a turning-point for the legal standing of the gospel.
15:
"But if it be a question of words and names, and of your law, look ye to it; for I will be no judge of such matters." The witnesses note Gallio's refusal to judge religious questions. On "words and names, and of your law," the witnesses note Gallio's perception. Ellicott: "It is Jewish, and not Roman law, which they are seeking to vindicate, and he will not make himself the executioner of an alien code." The dispute over whether Jesus is the Christ was, to Gallio, a matter of Jewish words and names.

On "look ye to it," the witnesses note the dismissal. Robertson: "I am not minded... An absolute refusal to allow a religious question to be brought before a Roman civil court." Gallio turns the matter back to the Jews to settle among themselves.

On the significance, Robertson notes: "This decision of Gallio does not establish Christianity in preference to Judaism. It simply means that the case was plainly that Christianity was a form of Judaism and as such was not opposed to Roman law. This decision opened the door for Paul's preaching all over the Roman Empire." Clarke praises Gallio's principle: "the state had no right to control any man's religious opinion."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Gallio refused to judge "a question of words and names, and of your law" — perceiving it was Jewish, not Roman law, and refusing to be the executioner of an alien code (Ellicott); an absolute refusal to allow a religious question in a Roman civil court (Robertson). The decision, by treating Christianity as a form of Judaism (a licensed religion), in effect opened the door for Paul's preaching throughout the empire (Robertson); and Clarke praises the principle that the state should not control religious opinion. The verse displays Gallio's momentous refusal: declining to judge a religious dispute, he unwittingly secured the legal toleration of the gospel. The verse marks the great judgment: the proconsul's dismissal of the Jewish charge as a matter of their own law. The verse displays the principle of religious liberty: the state has no business judging questions of doctrine and worship. Gallio's refusal, far from a mere personal indifference, established a precedent of immense importance: Christianity, treated as a form of Judaism, enjoyed the legal protection of a licensed religion, and Paul could preach freely across the empire. The verse displays God's providence using a pagan judge's clear-headedness to protect the gospel.
16:
"And he drave them from the judgment seat." The brief verse notes Gallio's dismissal of the Jews. The grammatical voices note the authoritative act. Barnes: "He refused to hear and decide the controversy. He commanded them to depart from the court... not that there was any violence used by Gallio, but merely that he dismissed them in an authoritative manner." Clarke: "an authoritative dismission."

The witnesses note the magisterial command. Ellicott: "The words imply a magisterial act. The order was given to the lictors to clear the court." Robertson: "The Jews were stunned by this sudden blow from the mild proconsul and wanted to linger to argue the case further, but they had to go."

The verse marks the decisive end of the trial: Gallio, having refused to judge the religious question, drives the Jews from the tribunal. Their assault on Paul has utterly failed.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Gallio drove the Jews from the judgment seat — an authoritative dismissal, not violent but magisterial (Barnes, Clarke), the lictors clearing the court (Ellicott); the Jews stunned by the sudden blow from the mild proconsul (Robertson). The verse displays the decisive end of the trial: the Jews dismissed, their charge rejected, Paul vindicated. The verse marks the failure of the Jewish assault: having brought Paul to the tribunal hoping for his condemnation, they are themselves driven from the court. The verse displays the completeness of Gallio's refusal: not merely declining to judge but dismissing the accusers. The Jews, who had hoped to use the Roman governor against Paul, found themselves rebuffed and expelled. The verse displays the providence that protected the gospel: the proconsul's firm dismissal of the malicious charge. The Jews' attempt to crush the gospel through Roman law had utterly failed; and the dismissal from the tribunal left them humiliated and the apostle free. The verse, brief as it is, marks the triumph of the gospel's legal standing: the charge rejected, the accusers driven out, the way opened for Paul's continued preaching. Gallio's firm hand, clearing the court, secured the apostle's freedom and the gospel's advance.
17:
"Then all the Greeks took Sosthenes, the chief ruler of the synagogue, and beat him before the judgment seat. And Gallio cared for none of those things." The witnesses note the beating of Sosthenes and the much-debated phrase about Gallio. On Sosthenes, the witnesses note he was likely the successor of Crispus, and probably became a Christian (the Sosthenes of 1 Corinthians 1:1). Barnes and Ellicott note the textual question (Greeks or Jews) but suppose he was beaten as the leader of the failed prosecution.

On "Gallio cared for none of those things," the witnesses strongly correct the common misreading. Barnes: "This has been usually charged on Gallio as a matter of reproach, as if he were wholly indifferent to religion. But the charge is unjustly made... All that this passage implies is that he did not deem it a part of his office to settle questions of a theological nature." Robertson: "These words have been often misunderstood as a description of Gallio's lack of interest in Christianity... But that is quite beside the mark. Gallio looked the other way while Sosthenes got the beating which he richly deserved." Clarke: "the words... only mean that he would not intermeddle in a controversy which did not belong to his province."

The Reformed Calvin, however, faults Gallio's carelessness: "by the mouth of Luke, the Spirit condemneth Gallio's carelessness, because he did not aid a man who was unjustly punished... let our magistrates know that they be far more inexcusable if they wink at injuries." The magistrate ought to protect the oppressed.

The witnesses agree: the crowd beat Sosthenes (the synagogue ruler, likely Crispus's successor, probably later a Christian, 1 Corinthians 1:1) before the tribunal, and "Gallio cared for none of those things." The witnesses divide on this phrase: most (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) strongly defend Gallio against the charge of religious indifference — the words mean only that he would not meddle in a Jewish quarrel outside his province, looking away from a police-court scuffle. Calvin, however, faults Gallio's carelessness in not protecting an oppressed man — the magistrate's duty to aid the wronged. The verse displays both the failure of the Jewish assault (their leader beaten) and the proconsul's detachment. The verse marks the misunderstood phrase "Gallio cared for none of those things": rightly read, not the indifference of a religious skeptic but the detachment of a judge declining to meddle in a Jewish brawl (Barnes, Robertson). The verse displays the danger of proof-texting: the phrase, often used to typify careless indifference to religion, in context means Gallio's refusal to be drawn into a dispute outside his jurisdiction. Yet Calvin's caution stands: a magistrate ought to protect the oppressed, and Gallio's indifference to Sosthenes's beating was a fault. The verse displays the complexity of Gallio: clear-headed in refusing the religious charge, yet careless in letting an injustice pass.
18:
"And Paul after this tarried there yet a good while, and then took his leave of the brethren, and sailed thence into Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila; having shorn his head in Cenchrea: for he had a vow." The witnesses note Paul's departure and the much-debated vow. On the vow, the witnesses divide on whose it was. The puritan Henry notes the ambiguity: "either Paul or Aquila (for the original does not determine which) had his head shaved, to discharge himself from the vow of a Nazarite." Most refer it to Paul.

On the nature and rightness of the vow, the witnesses debate. Chrysostom notes the accommodation: "See how the Law was breaking up; see how they were bound by conscience. This was a Jewish custom, to shear their heads agreeably with a vow." Ellicott justifies it as Paul being "all things to all men" (1 Corinthians 9:20): "A Nazarite vow would testify to all his brethren by blood that he did not despise the Law himself." He notes the vow may have grown from thankfulness for deliverance at Corinth, and the shearing at Cenchrea may have guarded against the appearance of effeminate long hair (1 Corinthians 11:14).

The witnesses note the place (Cenchrea, the eastern port of Corinth) and the companions (Priscilla and Aquila, sailing with Paul to Syria, Priscilla often named first). The vow was a Nazarite-like vow of devotion, lawfully discharged.

The witnesses agree: Paul, after a long stay, sailed for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila, having shorn his head at Cenchrea for a vow. The witnesses divide on whose vow it was: the Greek is ambiguous, possibly Paul's or Aquila's (Henry), though most refer it to Paul. They also weigh the rightness of Paul keeping a Jewish vow: Chrysostom notes the Law breaking up yet conscience still binding; Ellicott justifies it as Paul being "all things to all men" (1 Corinthians 9:20), testifying he did not despise the Law, the vow perhaps growing from thankfulness for deliverance, the shearing guarding against effeminate long hair. The verse displays Paul's accommodation to Jewish custom: a Nazarite-like vow, lawfully kept, showing he did not despise the Law. The verse marks Paul's departure from Corinth and the curious detail of the vow. The verse displays the apostle's principle of accommodation: in things indifferent, becoming as a Jew to the Jews, that he might win them — keeping a vow that testified his respect for the Law, while preaching freedom from it as a condition of salvation. The vow, whether Paul's or Aquila's, displays the lingering hold of Jewish piety in the early church and Paul's willingness to accommodate it in things indifferent, without compromising the gospel of grace.
19:
"And he came to Ephesus, and left them there: but he himself entered into the synagogue, and reasoned with the Jews." The witnesses note Paul's arrival at Ephesus and his leaving Aquila and Priscilla there. On Ephesus, the grammatical voices note the great city. Robertson: "this great city on the Cayster, the capital of the Province of Asia, the home of the worship of Diana (Artemis)... Paul at last had reached, though forbidden to come on the way out on this tour (16:6)." Paul would later spend three years here.

On leaving Aquila and Priscilla, the witnesses note Paul's purpose. Chrysostom: "these he left at Ephesus. With good reason, namely, that they should teach." They would instruct Apollos (v.26) and establish the work.

On reasoning in the synagogue, the witnesses note Paul's usual method. Robertson: "Luke's favourite word for Paul's synagogue discourses." Even on this brief visit, Paul reasoned with the Jews.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul came to Ephesus, the great capital of Asia and home of Diana's worship (Robertson), which he had been forbidden to enter earlier (16:6); he left Aquila and Priscilla there (that they might teach, Chrysostom) and reasoned in the synagogue. The verse displays Paul's brief but fruitful visit to Ephesus: reasoning with the Jews, leaving his fellow-workers to continue. The verse marks Paul's first contact with Ephesus, where he would later spend three years (ch. 19): a brief synagogue ministry, and the providential leaving of Aquila and Priscilla, who would instruct Apollos. The verse displays the providence in Paul's movements: leaving his trusted companions at Ephesus, where they would do great good (teaching Apollos, establishing the work) before Paul's return. The verse displays both Paul's diligence (reasoning in the synagogue even on a brief visit) and his strategic placing of fellow-workers (leaving Aquila and Priscilla at the key city of Ephesus). The brief visit sowed seeds that would bear fruit: the synagogue reasoning, the leaving of capable teachers, and the preparation for the great Ephesian ministry to come. Paul's reaching of Ephesus, long desired and earlier forbidden, marks a significant stage in the gospel's westward advance.
20:
"When they desired him to tarry longer time with them, he consented not;" The brief verse notes the Ephesians' request and Paul's refusal. The grammatical voices note the hopeful sign. Ellicott: "This was, obviously, a hopeful sign, the earnest of the fruitful labours that followed. Nowhere among the churches that he founded does St. Paul seem to have found so great a receptivity for spiritual truth." The Ephesians' eagerness foreshadowed the great Ephesian ministry.

On Paul's refusal, the witnesses note his determination to reach Jerusalem. Robertson: "He consented not." Paul, eager to keep the feast (v.21), declined to stay, despite the Ephesians' welcome.

The witnesses note the contrast with other cities. Ellicott contrasts the receptive Ephesians with the Corinthians (still babes, 1 Corinthians 3:2). Paul's later three-year ministry at Ephesus, and his profound epistle to them, would fulfill this early promise.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Ephesians asked Paul to stay longer, but he consented not — their eagerness a hopeful sign of the great receptivity Paul found at Ephesus (Ellicott), foreshadowing the fruitful three-year ministry to come. Paul declined, determined to reach Jerusalem for the feast (v.21). The verse displays the Ephesians' openness and Paul's resolve: their welcome was warm, but his purpose drew him on. The verse marks the promise of Ephesus: a receptive people, eager for the gospel, whom Paul would later serve at length. The verse displays both the warmth of the Ephesian welcome and Paul's firm purpose to keep his planned journey. The Ephesians' request, far from being a rejection, was an earnest of the fruitful labors that would follow — Paul's later three-year ministry, and the profound truths of the Epistle to the Ephesians. The verse displays Paul's discipline: even where he was warmly welcomed and could have stayed, he kept to his purpose, trusting to return (v.21). The brief refusal, with its promise to come back, displays the apostle's strategic mind: he would return to Ephesus, where the soil was so receptive, but first must complete his present journey. The eager Ephesians would not be disappointed; Paul would return and labor among them long.
21:
"But bade them farewell, saying, I must by all means keep this feast that cometh in Jerusalem: but I will return again unto you, if God will. And he sailed from Ephesus." The witnesses note Paul's farewell, his purpose to keep the feast, and his pious "if God will." On keeping the feast, the witnesses note Paul's reason. Barnes: "he wished to meet as many of his countrymen as possible, and to remove, if practicable, the prejudices against him." Wesley: "to take the opportunity of meeting a great number of his countrymen to whom he might preach Christ." (The witnesses note the clause is textually doubtful in some MSS.)

On "if God will," the witnesses note the pious submission. Ellicott: "In this resting in the thought of the will of the Father as ordering all things well... we find another point of agreement between St. Paul and St. James (James 4:15)." Robertson: "a recognition that we are in God's hands." Paul subjects even his plans to God's will.

The witnesses note Paul's promise to return, which he fulfilled (19:1). The brief Ephesian visit ends with a farewell and a promise, the apostle sailing on toward Jerusalem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul bade the Ephesians farewell, determined to keep the feast at Jerusalem (to meet and win his countrymen, Barnes, Wesley — the clause textually doubtful in some MSS), promising to return "if God will" — the pious submission of his plans to God's will (Ellicott, Robertson, James 4:15). He sailed from Ephesus, fulfilling the promise to return at 19:1. The verse displays Paul's piety and purpose: subjecting his plans to God's will, yet pressing on to keep the feast and win his countrymen. The verse marks the close of the brief Ephesian visit: a farewell, a purpose, and a pious "if God will." The verse displays the apostle's submission to providence: even his firm plans are held under God's will, the model of Christian planning (James 4:15). The "if God will" is no empty formula but a genuine resting in the Father's ordering of all things. The verse displays both Paul's missionary zeal (keeping the feast to reach the Jews) and his humble dependence (his plans subject to God). The promise to return, fulfilled at 19:1, displays his faithfulness and the providence that brought him back to the receptive Ephesians. Paul's farewell, with its pious submission and faithful promise, marks the apostle's character: zealous yet humble, purposeful yet dependent on God.
22:
"And when he had landed at Caesarea, and gone up, and saluted the church, he went down to Antioch." The witnesses note Paul's journey to Jerusalem and Antioch, ending the second journey. On "gone up," the witnesses note this means going up to Jerusalem. Clarke and Robertson note the idiom: "gone up" to Jerusalem (the customary phrase), and then "went down" to Antioch — the language suiting a visit to Jerusalem, not merely Caesarea. Robertson: "Here Paul saluted the church in the fourth of his five visits after his conversion."

On the visit, the witnesses note its brevity. Calvin: "it is certain that he was drawn thither with some great necessity... he stayed not long at Jerusalem." Ellicott suggests the completion of the Nazarite vow in the temple, and a welcome from James and the elders.

On the return to Antioch, the witnesses note the close of the second journey. Robertson: "he returned to Antioch to make a report of his second mission tour as he had done at the close of the first (14:26-28)." Paul came back to his sending church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul landed at Caesarea, went up (to Jerusalem, the customary idiom, Clarke, Robertson) and saluted the church (his fourth post-conversion visit, Robertson), then went down to Antioch — closing the second missionary journey. The visit to Jerusalem was brief (Calvin), perhaps completing the Nazarite vow in the temple (Ellicott). The verse displays the close of the second journey: Paul reporting to Jerusalem and Antioch, his sending church. The verse marks the end of the great second missionary journey: Paul, having founded churches across Macedonia and Achaia, returns to greet the mother church and his home base. The verse displays the apostle's faithfulness in reporting: as after the first journey (14:26-28), Paul returns to Antioch to recount God's work. The terse language ("gone up... went down") covers a significant circuit: the visit to Jerusalem, the completion of the vow, the return to Antioch. The verse displays the rhythm of the apostolic mission: journey and return, labor and report. Paul's coming home to Antioch closes the second journey and prepares for the third (v.23). The brief notice of a long and significant journey displays Luke's economy: the great second journey, with all its triumphs at Philippi, Thessalonica, Athens, and Corinth, ends quietly with Paul greeting the churches and returning home.
23:
"And after he had spent some time there, he departed, and went over all the country of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples." The witnesses note the beginning of the third missionary journey. The grammatical voices note Paul's solitary start. Robertson: "Thus simply and alone Paul began the third mission tour without a Barnabas or a Silas." He revisited the churches of Galatia and Phrygia.

On "in order," the witnesses note the systematic visitation. Clarke: "in order, distinctly, particularly... as opposed to confusion." Paul went through the churches one by one, strengthening them.

On "strengthening all the disciples," the witnesses note the pastoral purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the same verb (or its compound) as on the second journey (15:41): confirming and establishing the believers. Ellicott notes this is the visit reflected in the Epistle to the Galatians, before the Judaizers had done their damage.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after a stay at Antioch, Paul began the third journey, going through Galatia and Phrygia "in order" (systematically, Clarke), strengthening all the disciples — beginning alone, without Barnabas or Silas (Robertson), revisiting the churches he had founded. The pastoral purpose was to confirm and establish the believers (Barnes, Robertson, as 15:41); Ellicott notes this is the visit reflected in Galatians, before the Judaizers' damage. The verse displays the beginning of the third missionary journey: Paul's systematic, pastoral visitation of the Galatian and Phrygian churches. The verse marks the opening of the great third journey, which would include the long Ephesian ministry (ch. 19): Paul, revisiting his churches, strengthening the disciples. The verse displays the apostle's pastoral heart: not content with founding churches, he returns to confirm them. The systematic visitation ("in order") displays his care and method: going through the churches one by one, establishing each. The verse displays the continuity of Paul's ministry: the third journey beginning, like the second, with the strengthening of existing churches before pressing on to new fields. Paul's solitary start (without Barnabas or Silas) displays his perseverance; and his strengthening of the disciples displays the goal of all his labor — not merely to win converts but to build them up. The third journey opens with the pastoral confirming of the churches Paul had planted.
24:
"And a certain Jew named Apollos, born at Alexandria, an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures, came to Ephesus." The witnesses note the introduction of Apollos. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note his gifts: "He was an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures of the Old Testament." A Jew of Alexandria (the great center of Hellenistic Judaism, home of Philo), he was learned and gifted.

On "eloquent," the witnesses note the rare gift of eloquence joined to scriptural knowledge. Apollos combined the rhetorical training of Alexandria with deep knowledge of the Old Testament — a powerful preacher.

On "mighty in the scriptures," the witnesses note his scriptural power. He was able to expound and apply the Old Testament with force, particularly (v.28) to prove that Jesus is the Christ. The verse introduces a figure who would become prominent at Corinth (1 Corinthians 1-3).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Apollos, a Jew of Alexandria, eloquent and mighty in the Scriptures, came to Ephesus — combining the rhetorical training of Alexandria (home of Philo) with deep knowledge of the Old Testament (Henry). The verse displays the introduction of a remarkable figure: a learned, eloquent, scripturally powerful preacher. The verse marks the entrance of Apollos, who would labor at Corinth (1 Corinthians 1-3) and become, with Paul and Peter, a figure around whom the Corinthians formed parties. The verse displays the diversity of gifts in the early church: Apollos's eloquence and scriptural might complemented Paul's labor. The verse introduces a man of unusual endowments — eloquent in speech, mighty in the Scriptures — whose gifts would greatly help the believers (v.27) and confute the Jews (v.28). Yet, as the next verses show, even this gifted man had something to learn (v.25), and was humble enough to be instructed (v.26). The verse displays the rising of learned and gifted men in the church, as Chrysostom notes: "even learned men are now urgent, and the disciples henceforth go abroad." Apollos's coming to Ephesus, where Aquila and Priscilla awaited, was providential: there he would be more perfectly instructed before his fruitful ministry at Corinth.
25:
"This man was instructed in the way of the Lord; and being fervent in the spirit, he spake and taught diligently the things of the Lord, knowing only the baptism of John." The witnesses note Apollos's instruction, fervor, and his limited knowledge. On "instructed in the way of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the term (catechized). Robertson: "to teach by repeated dinning into the ears... to teach orally by word of mouth." Apollos had been taught accurately what he knew.

On "fervent in the spirit," the witnesses note his zeal. Ellicott and Robertson note "the spirit" is the man's own spirit (as Romans 12:11): Apollos was boiling, fervent, zealous in teaching.

On "knowing only the baptism of John," the witnesses note his limited knowledge. Apollos knew the facts of Jesus's life and accepted him as the Christ, but his knowledge stopped at John's baptism — he had not learned of the Cross, the Resurrection, and Pentecost in their fullness (Robertson, Ellicott). The Reformed Calvin notes the value of zeal joined to knowledge: "doctrine without zeal is either like a sword in the hand of a madman, or else it lieth still as cold... Therefore, that doctrine shall be unsavory which is not joined with zeal. But let us remember that Luke putteth the knowledge of the Scripture in the first place, which must be the moderation of zeal."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Apollos was instructed (catechized) in the way of the Lord, fervent in spirit (his own zealous spirit, Romans 12:11, Ellicott, Robertson), teaching diligently the things of the Lord, yet "knowing only the baptism of John" — accurate in what he knew, but his knowledge stopping short of the Cross, Resurrection, and Pentecost (Robertson). Calvin notes the value of zeal joined to knowledge: doctrine without zeal is cold, but zeal must be governed by knowledge of the Scripture. The verse displays a gifted but incompletely instructed teacher: fervent, accurate as far as he knew, yet needing fuller instruction. The verse marks the curious case of Apollos: eloquent and zealous, teaching accurately, yet knowing only John's baptism — accepting Jesus as the Christ but ignorant of the full gospel. The verse displays the combination of gifts and limitations: Apollos's eloquence and fervor were real, but his knowledge needed completing (v.26). The verse teaches that even gifted teachers may have gaps in their knowledge, and that zeal must be joined to and governed by accurate knowledge (Calvin). Apollos's fervor, accurate teaching, and humble teachableness (v.26) display the model of a true teacher: zealous, faithful to what he knows, and open to learn more.
26:
"And he began to speak boldly in the synagogue: whom when Aquila and Priscilla had heard, they took him unto them, and expounded unto him the way of God more perfectly." The witnesses note Apollos's bold preaching and his instruction by Aquila and Priscilla — including the notable point of a woman teaching. On the instruction, the grammatical voices note the gentle correction. Robertson: "Instead of abusing the young and brilliant preacher for his ignorance they (particularly Priscilla) gave him the fuller story... to fill up the gaps in his knowledge. It is a needed and delicate task, this thing of teaching gifted young ministers."

On Priscilla's role, the witnesses note the woman teaching. Clarke: "This eloquent man, and mighty in the Scriptures, who was even a public teacher, was not ashamed to be indebted to the instructions of a Christian woman." The Reformed Calvin notes the propriety and the limit: "we see that at that time women were not so ignorant of the word of God as the Papists will have them... Notwithstanding, we must remember that Priscilla did execute this function of teaching at home in her own house, that she might not overthrow the order prescribed by God and nature." Private instruction, not public teaching.

The witnesses note the humility of all parties: Aquila and Priscilla instructing privately and lovingly; Apollos, the eloquent scholar, humbly receiving instruction from a tradesman and his wife (Calvin).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Apollos preached boldly in the synagogue, and Aquila and Priscilla, hearing him, took him aside and expounded the way of God more perfectly — a gentle, delicate correction of a gifted young preacher (Robertson), filling the gaps in his knowledge. The notable point is Priscilla's role in teaching: Apollos, the eloquent scholar, humbly received instruction from a Christian woman and her husband (Clarke); Calvin notes women were not as ignorant as the Papists claim, yet Priscilla taught privately at home, not overthrowing the order of public teaching. The verse displays the loving, private instruction of a gifted but incomplete teacher: Aquila and Priscilla not abusing but helping Apollos. The verse marks the completion of Apollos's knowledge: the way of God expounded more perfectly. The verse displays both the humility of Apollos (the eloquent scholar receiving instruction from a tradesman and his wife) and the wisdom of his teachers (correcting privately and lovingly). The verse touches the question of women teaching: Priscilla helped instruct Apollos, yet privately, not in public office (Calvin) — a model of how knowledge may be shared without overthrowing church order. The verse displays the beauty of mutual ministry: the gifted helped by the humble, the eloquent taught by the lowly, all for the building up of the church.
27:
"And when he was disposed to pass into Achaia, the brethren wrote, exhorting the disciples to receive him: who, when he was come, helped them much which had believed through grace:" The witnesses note Apollos's move to Achaia (Corinth), the letter of commendation, and his help to the believers. On the letter, the grammatical voices note the first letter of commendation. Ellicott: "This is the first instance of what were afterwards known technically as 'letters of commendation' (2 Corinthians 3:1), written by one church to another in favour of the bearer." The Ephesian brethren commended Apollos to the Corinthians.

On Apollos helping the believers, the witnesses note his ministry. Barnes: "Helped them much... Strengthened them, and aided them in their controversies with the unbelieving Jews." Now more fully instructed, Apollos powerfully aided the Corinthian church.

On "through grace," the witnesses note the phrase may go with "helped" or "believed." Robertson: "This makes sense if taken with 'believed'... or with 'helped.' Both are true." Whether the believers believed through grace, or Apollos helped through grace, all is of God's grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Apollos, disposed to go to Achaia (Corinth), was commended by the Ephesian brethren in a letter — the first instance of a "letter of commendation" (2 Corinthians 3:1, Ellicott) — and helped the believers much, strengthening them and aiding them against the Jews (Barnes). The "through grace" may go with "helped" or "believed" — both true, all of God's grace (Robertson). The verse displays the spread of Apollos's ministry: commended to Corinth, where he greatly helped the church. The verse marks Apollos's move to Corinth, where he would water what Paul had planted (1 Corinthians 3:6): a powerful aid to the believers. The verse displays the practice of letters of commendation, the early church's way of vouching for traveling teachers. The verse displays the fruit of Apollos's completed instruction: now fully equipped, he greatly helped the Corinthian believers, strengthening them in faith and against their opponents. The phrase "through grace," whether applied to the believers' faith or Apollos's help, ascribes all to God: it is by grace that any believe, and by grace that any gift profits the church. Apollos, commended and equipped, became a great help to the church Paul had founded — the planter and the waterer, both serving the one God who gives the increase.
28:
"For he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publicly, shewing by the scriptures that Jesus was Christ." The witnesses note Apollos's powerful public confutation of the Jews. On "mightily convinced," the grammatical voices note the force. Gill: "His reasoning was so strong and nervous, his arguments so weighty and powerful, and the passages he produced out of the Old Testament so full and pertinent, that the Jews were not able to stand against him." Barnes: "he bore down all opposition, and effectually silenced them."

On "publicly," the witnesses note the open debate. Apollos confuted the Jews not in private but in public disputation, openly proving his case.

On "shewing by the scriptures that Jesus was Christ," the witnesses note his method. Like Paul, Apollos proved from the Old Testament that Jesus is the Messiah. His eloquence and scriptural might (v.24), now completed by fuller instruction (v.26), made him a formidable champion of the gospel. The verse displays the fruit of his gifts and his teachableness: a mighty public confuter of the Jews.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Apollos mightily and publicly confuted the Jews, showing by the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ — his reasoning strong, his arguments weighty, his scriptural proofs irresistible (Gill, Barnes), bearing down all opposition. He confuted them openly, proving from the Old Testament that Jesus is the Messiah, like Paul. The verse displays the fruit of Apollos's gifts and teachableness: a mighty public champion of the gospel. The verse marks the close of the chapter with Apollos's powerful ministry: the eloquent, scripturally mighty Alexandrian, now fully instructed, confuting the Jews and proving Jesus the Christ. The verse displays the value of combined gifts: eloquence, scriptural knowledge, fervor, and teachableness, all serving the gospel. Apollos, who had known only John's baptism (v.25) but humbly received fuller instruction (v.26), became a formidable defender of the faith. The verse displays the goal of all his preparation: the public, powerful proof that Jesus is the Christ, silencing the Jews from their own Scriptures. The chapter that began with Paul at Corinth ends with Apollos at Corinth, the planter and the waterer both serving the one harvest. Apollos's mighty confutation of the Jews, proving Christ from the Scriptures, displays the triumph of the gospel through gifted and teachable servants.
